did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a modified EfficientNet-B0 deep learning architecture optimized with a quantum-behaved particle swarm optimization (QBPSO) algorithm to perform skin cancer lesion classification on dermoscopic images from the HAM10000, ISIC 2019, and MSLD v2.0 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a modified EfficientNet-B0 architecture optimized with a quantum-behaved particle swarm optimization (QBPSO) algorithm. It reports new results from comprehensive simulations on public datasets (HAM10000, ISIC 2019, MSLD v2.0), including specific performance metrics like 99.62% accuracy and computational times, and compares these against state-of-the-art models. The structure includes standard research sections such as Methods, Results, and Conclusions, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Modified EfficientNet-B0 Architecture Optimized with Quantum-Behaved Algorithm for Skin Cancer Lesion Assessment
brief_summary: This study addresses skin cancer lesion classification by developing a modified EfficientNet-B0 architecture optimized with a quantum-behaved particle swarm optimization (QBPSO) algorithm. The model was evaluated on dermoscopic images from the HAM10000, ISIC 2019, and MSLD v2.0 datasets, utilizing SMOTE to handle class imbalance. Comprehensive simulations demonstrated a mean accuracy of 99.62% on HAM10000 and 92.5% on ISIC 2019, outperforming state-of-the-art pre-trained models and existing techniques. The reliability and stability of the proposed framework were further validated through Monte Carlo simulations of 100 independent runs. The results indicate that the optimized architecture effectively reduces diagnostic errors and assists dermatologists in clinical decision-making despite challenges like data imbalance.

lesion_types_studied.details: Precancerous/cancerous lesion class in HAM10000 dataset; 327 original images, balanced to 2000 via SMOTE.
Common skin cancer class; 514 images in HAM10000, balanced to 2000 via SMOTE; also present in ISIC 2019.
Includes seborrheic keratoses; 1099 images in HAM10000, balanced to 2000 via SMOTE; also present in ISIC 2019.
Harmless fibrous nodule; 115 images in HAM10000, balanced to 2000 via SMOTE; also present in ISIC 2019.
Most dangerous skin cancer; 1113 images in HAM10000, balanced to 2000 via SMOTE; also present in ISIC 2019.
Benign lesion; 67 original images in HAM10000 (largest class after SMOTE); dominant class in ISIC 2019 with 12,875 images, not balanced by SMOTE.
Benign lesion; 142 images in HAM10000, balanced to 2000 via SMOTE; also present in ISIC 2019.
Malignant lesion; present in ISIC 2019 dataset.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (e.g., AKIC, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, MV, VL in HAM10000; ActK, BaCC, BeKev, DerF, Mel, MN, SCC, VasL in ISIC 2019; Mpox, Chipox, Mea, Cowpox, HFMD, Healthy in MSLD v2.0).
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin cancer lesions and other dermatological conditions using a modified EfficientNet-B0 architecture. The model predicts discrete class labels for dermoscopic images. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, specificity, and confusion matrices. The loss function used is standard classification cross-entropy with label smoothing. Specific tasks include: 1) 7-class classification on HAM10000 (balanced via SMOTE), 2) 8-class classification on ISIC 2019, and 3) 6-class classification on MSLD v2.0.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset of dermatoscopic images for skin cancer analysis, containing seven classes of pigmented skin lesions. The original dataset had 10,015 images but was processed using SMOTE to handle class imbalance.

use.training: Used for training the modified EfficientNet-B0 architecture optimized with QBPSO. 70% of the SMOTE-balanced data was used for training.
use.testing: Used for testing the framework's classification performance. 30% of the data was reserved for testing.
use.other: The dataset was originally imbalanced; SMOTE was applied to generate synthetic samples for rare classes (AKIC, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, VL) to reach 2000 images per class, while MV remained unbalanced.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A prominent and publicly recognized benchmark dataset for skin lesion classification, containing eight distinct classes of dermoscopic images. It is noted for being notoriously imbalanced.

use.training: Used for training the model. 70% of the SMOTE-processed data was used for training.
use.testing: Used for testing the framework. 30% of the data was reserved for testing.
use.other: SMOTE was applied to balance classes to 4000 images per class, except for the dominant MN class which remained at its original count.

datasets_used.name: MSLD v2.0
datasets_used.description: A dataset containing six different classes of skin lesions including viral infections and healthy skin, used to examine the strength of the proposed architecture on diverse conditions.

use.training: Used for training the model. 70% of the data was used for training.
use.testing: Used for testing the framework. 30% of the data was reserved for testing.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To examine the reliability of the proposed architecture and stability of the QBPSO algorithm through 100 independent runs.
To evaluate the overall performance and robustness of the stochastic-based architecture across independent runs.
To evaluate the overall performance and robustness of the stochastic-based architecture across independent runs.
To evaluate the overall performance and robustness of the stochastic-based architecture across independent runs.
To evaluate the overall performance and robustness of the stochastic-based architecture across independent runs.
To analyze the distribution shape of the results from independent runs.
To demonstrate reliable classification accuracy and balance optimization precision with robustness.
Used as a hyperparameter setting for the MENet-B0 model optimization (specifically 10-fold).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., t-tests, ANOVA with p-values) to compare models. Instead, it used Monte Carlo simulations of 100 independent runs to evaluate reliability and stability, reporting descriptive statistics such as mean, variance, standard deviation, kurtosis, and Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MaCC), along with confidence intervals, but no significance tests or p-values were provided.

HAM10000.metric: Accuracy, FNR, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, Specificity, FPR
HAM10000.notes: The model achieved an overall accuracy of 99.62% with only 0.38% error. Per-class accuracy ranged between 99.45% and 99.81%. Precision and recall values remained above 0.99 for every class. The MV class achieved the highest F1 score (0.9984) and lowest FNR (0.0019).

ISIC 2019.metric: Accuracy, FNR, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, Specificity, FPR
ISIC 2019.notes: The model correctly classified 37,811 instances while misclassifying 3064. BaCC attained the highest accuracy of 95.53%, followed by VasL at 95.67%. MN showed the weakest performance at 90.66%. BeKev and MN exhibited lower recall values.

MSLD v2.0.metric: Accuracy, FNR, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, Specificity, FPR
MSLD v2.0.notes: Mpox, HFMD, and Healthy classes achieved near-perfect precision, recall, and F1 scores. Chipox, Mea, and Cowpox demonstrated high accuracy with precision and recall values above 0.93.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not explicitly reported; described as a modified architecture developed based on mobile inverted bottleneck convolution with squeeze and excitation approach.

architecture_details.backbone: Mobile Inverted Bottleneck Convolution (MBConv) blocks with squeeze and excitation (SqEx) approach
architecture_details.kernel_sizes: 3 × 3 and 5 × 5 kernels in MBConv blocks; 1 × 1 bottleneck convolution
special_modules: Initial 3 × 3 convolutional layer for low-level features
Self-attention mechanism for selective focus on discriminative regions

training_details.loss_function: Standard classification cross-entropy with label smoothing

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected dense layer followed by Softmax activation function

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: MENet-B0 optimized with Quantum-behaved Particle Swarm Optimization (QBPSO)
MENet-B0 optimized with Standard Particle Swarm Optimization (S-PSO)
preprocessing_techniques: SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) to balance class distribution in HAM10000 and ISIC 2019 datasets
Standardization of image resolution to 224 × 224 pixels
Standardization of bit depth to 24 bits
Data splitting into 70% training and 30% testing sets

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to handle class imbalance in HAM10000 and ISIC 2019 datasets. For HAM10000, rare classes (AKIC, BCC, BKL, DF, MEL, VL) were balanced to 2000 images per class, resulting in 8690 synthetic images generated. For ISIC 2019, classes were balanced to 4000 images per class, except for the dominant MN class which remained at its original count of 12,875. No augmentation was applied to MSLD v2.0.

conclusions: The proposed modified EfficientNet-B0 (MENet-B0) architecture optimized with the quantum-behaved particle swarm optimization (QBPSO) algorithm significantly outperforms state-of-the-art pre-trained models and existing techniques in skin cancer lesion classification.

The framework achieved a mean accuracy of 99.62% on the HAM10000 dataset and 92.5% on the ISIC 2019 dataset, demonstrating superior performance compared to baseline EfficientNet variants and other reported methods.

The use of QBPSO for hyperparameter optimization resulted in lower misclassification rates (0.38% on HAM10000 and 7.50% on ISIC 2019) and better fitness values compared to standard PSO (S-PSO) and other optimizers.

Statistical analysis via Monte Carlo simulations of 100 independent runs confirmed the reliability and stability of the proposed architecture, with QBPSO showing statistically significant superiority over S-PSO (p=0.0153).

The model effectively handles class imbalance issues, particularly in the HAM10000 dataset, by maintaining high precision and recall across all lesion classes, although some classes like MN and BeKev in ISIC 2019 remained more challenging.

Despite high diagnostic accuracy, the study acknowledges limitations including the 'black box' nature of deep learning models which reduces clinical trust, the domain shift between controlled dermoscopic images and real-world clinical conditions, and the high computational resources required for real-time deployment.

The proposed framework has the potential to reduce diagnostic errors and assist dermatologists in clinical decision-making for improved patient outcomes, with future work planned for implementation on embedded hardware.
important_remarks: The study relies on SMOTE to artificially balance class distributions in HAM10000 and ISIC 2019, which may not reflect real-world clinical prevalence and could lead to over-optimistic performance estimates.
The MV (Melanocytic nevus) class in HAM10000 was not balanced by SMOTE, remaining the dominant class (6705 images vs 2000 for others), potentially biasing the model towards this class.
In the ISIC 2019 dataset, the MN class remained unbalanced (12,875 images) while other classes were balanced to 4000, creating a significant class imbalance that may affect generalizability.

The MSLD v2.0 dataset used for validation is small (755 total images) and contains viral lesions (Mpox, Chickenpox, etc.) rather than typical skin cancer classes, limiting the assessment of the model's primary dermatological utility.
The paper acknowledges the 'black box' nature of deep learning models as a limitation that reduces clinical trust and interpretability.
The study notes a potential domain shift between controlled dermoscopic images used in training and real-world clinical conditions, which may impact deployment effectiveness.

High computational resources are required for real-time deployment, as indicated by the mean computational times (501.431 s for HAM10000 and 752.421 s for ISIC 2019) reported for the optimized architecture.
No external validation on independent, unseen clinical datasets was performed; validation was limited to public benchmark datasets (HAM10000, ISIC 2019, MSLD v2.0).

The paper does not report formal hypothesis testing with p-values for model comparisons, relying instead on Monte Carlo simulations of 100 independent runs and descriptive statistics (mean, variance, standard deviation) to establish reliability.
The authors declare no conflicts of interest, but the study was supported by Johns Hopkins Aramco Healthcare, which may introduce affiliation bias.
